words.hint: Arrangement of natural and artificial features of an area
words.educational: Topography describes the surface features of a place including elevation, slope, and landforms, crucial for mapping and land use planning.

words.hint: Study of landforms and earth surface processes
words.educational: Geomorphology studies how landforms develop through processes like erosion, weathering, and tectonic activity over geological time.

words.hint: Science and art of making maps
words.educational: Cartography combines science, aesthetics, and technique to create maps. Modern cartography uses GIS and satellite technology.

words.hint: Variety of life in a particular habitat
words.educational: Biodiversity refers to the variety of life forms in an ecosystem. Tropical rainforests have the highest biodiversity on Earth.

words.hint: Related to large landmasses of continents
words.educational: Continental refers to characteristics of continents. Continental drift theory explains how continents moved over millions of years.

words.hint: Clearing forests for other land uses
words.educational: Deforestation is the permanent removal of forests, causing habitat loss, climate change, and soil erosion. Amazon faces severe deforestation.

words.hint: Growth of cities and shift from rural living
words.educational: Urbanization is the increasing proportion of people living in cities. Over 55% of the world's population now lives in urban areas.

words.hint: Water falling from atmosphere as rain or snow
words.educational: Precipitation includes rain, snow, sleet, and hail. Distribution of precipitation determines climate zones and ecosystems.
Seismology studies earthquakes and seismic waves. Seismographs detect and measure earthquakes, helping predict future events.

words.hint: Community of organisms interacting with environment
words.educational: An ecosystem includes all living organisms and their physical environment interacting as a system through energy and nutrient cycles.
